content:
@Alice Ryhl said in mailing list:

I would just add a default implementation in the trait declaration. That
way, you can avoid adding the method to every implementation.

What does he mean by adding default implementation in the trait declaration?

issue: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1057
PR: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/1059
mailing list: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/d5679da6-59a1-4b2e-83af-3ab82e517f0f@ryhl.io/T/#t

Hi, a default implementation is an implementation of a trait method inside the trait definition. Any type that implements the trait and does not supply an implementation of a method with a default implementation will use the default implementation. Also have a look here: https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/book/ch10-02-traits.html#default-implementations

Obei Sideg said:

Alice Ryhl said in mailing list:

I would just add a default implementation in the trait declaration. That
way, you can avoid adding the method to every implementation.

What does he mean by adding default implementation in the trait declaration?

*She

Thanks @Valentin Obst
I have done the fix, should i send it as new patch or just replay to the current email?

You should send a v2 patch, makes it easier to follow (include a link to the v1 patch and a change note after the --- and before the diff in the commit message, like https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240116151728.370238-1-mcanal@igalia.com/)
Also, please drop reviewed-bys when there are nontrivial changes across versions

error: call to unsafe function is unsafe and requires unsafe block (error E0133)
  --> rust/kernel/types.rs:65:18
   |
65 |             Some(Self::from_foreign(ptr))
   |                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ call to unsafe function
   |
   = note: consult the function's documentation for information on how to avoid undefined behavior
note: an unsafe function restricts its caller, but its body is safe by default
  --> rust/kernel/types.rs:61:5
   |
61 |     unsafe fn try_from_foreign(ptr: *const core::ffi::c_void) -> Option<Self> {
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   = note: requested on the command line with `-D unsafe-op-in-unsafe-fn`

error: aborting due to previous error

Your patch currently does not compile. The reason is that (contrary to Rust's defaults) unsafe operations inside unsafe functions still need to be inside an unsafe block, i.e., wrap the call to from_foreign into an unsafe block.
I am searching for documentation to build my code. Can someone please share the link for the documentation?

Obei Sideg said:

I am searching for documentation to build my code. Can someone please share the link for the documentation?

https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/rust/quick-start.html

i.e., wrap the call to from_foreign into an unsafe block.

And don't forget to add a SAFETY comment explaining why the preconditions of the unsafe operation are satisfied.
How to build a specific part (the rust directory)?
In general, when submitting a patch, please build the kernel normally to be sure that callers can still work with whatever changes you made.
I tried to run make LLVM=1, and the kernel built successfully even though I included code that does not compile.
what i am doing wrong?
You need to adjust your config file
You can run make LLVM=1 menuconfig and then enable Rust support
From which section can I enable Rust?
I think it's just called general
Near the bottom
You mean General setup?
Yes

Nothing inside general mention rust
menu-general.png
Then you have not properly set up your Rust compiler. Please read Documentation/rust/quick-start.rst
FYI in menuconfig you can search RUST (or anything) to see what its dependencies are that aren't enabled
I think you need to turn off modversions now which I don't see is in the documentation

Trevor Gross said:

I think you need to turn off modversions now which I don't see is in the documentation

I don't think we should put dependencies in the documentation -- we would be duplicating Kconfig and, as you say, one can use menuconfig to inspect whether they are enabled or not etc.
Oh yeah, I wasn't saying that it should be

/// Tries to convert a foreign-owned object back to a Rust-owned one.
///
/// A convenience wrapper over [`from_foreign`] that returns [`None`] if `ptr` is null.
///
/// # Safety
///
/// Assumes `ptr` is a non-null pointer from a prior `Self::into_foreign` call.
/// Users must ensure this requirement, and handle the result with care to avoid UB.
/// Returns `None` if `ptr` is null.
unsafe fn try_from_foreign(ptr: *const core::ffi::c_void) -> Option<Self> {
        if ptr.is_null() {
            None
        } else {
            // SAFETY: The safety requirements of this function ensure that `ptr` comes from a previous
            // call to `Self::into_foreign`.
            unsafe { Some(Self::from_foreign(ptr)) }
        }
    }

@Alice Ryhl Does this look okay?
The wording "assumes ptris a non-null pointer" means that calling it with a null pointer is not allowed.
You need to instead say that either it is null, or it came from a prior call to into_foreign.

/// Tries to convert a foreign-owned object back to a Rust-owned one.
///
/// A convenience wrapper over [`from_foreign`] that returns [`None`] if `ptr` is null.
///
/// # Safety
///
/// Assumes `ptr` is either null or from a prior `Self::into_foreign` call.
/// Users must ensure this requirement, and handle the result with care to avoid UB.
/// Returns `None` if `ptr` is null.
unsafe fn try_from_foreign(ptr: *const core::ffi::c_void) -> Option<Self> {

How about this?
Saying that you get UB if you don't follow the safety requirements is redundant. That's what "# Safety" means.
The null case is already covered in the second paragraph. The Safety section should only be concerned with pointing out that ptr should be from a prior into_foreign call.
You can't have a safety comment that requires that the pointer is non-null when it's allowed to be null.

/// Assumes `ptr` is either null or came from a prior call to `Self::into_foreign`.
/// Returns `None` if `ptr` is null.

You don't need to specify that it returns None if ptr is null in the safety comment. The safety comment is for listing the requirements the caller must follow to avoid UB. "Returns None if null" is not a requirement that the caller must follow to avoid UB.
/// Assumes `ptr` is either null or came from a prior call to [`into_foreign`].

I would use the same wording as from_foreign, or refer to it like this:

/// `ptr` must either be null or satisfy the safety requirements for [`from_foreign`].
Also, you shouldn't send another version now immediately.
Wait for others to review it.
You can respond to me saying that you intend to make that change.
Then I will respond that it looks good to me, and that you may add my Reviewed-by if you make the change.
The general etiquette is to wait for conversation to stop before sending another version.

@Obei Sideg your emails have been coming with a References: header, which makes lore think that you're replying to an email that it can't find. E.g. at https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/2024012945-chrome-hamstring-6784@gregkh/T/#m9b58e7e94f3fa3a6e906e6702b0a2d8019cd00eb

image.png

Any clue why? Maybe your email provider is adding it for whatever reason
I am not sure why, I will check that
